---
title: "The Rural Mobilization Gap"
subtitle: "Intermediary selection vs. market structure in the U.S. New Markets Tax Credit"
author: "Dr. Ian Helfrich"
date: last-modified
---

The U.S. New Markets Tax Credit, enacted in 2000, is the largest place-based
tax-credit program in the United States. Over its first two decades it
deployed **\$66.6 billion of federal-credit-subsidized capital** into
**8,024 projects** whose combined cost was **\$120.9 billion**, implying
a program-wide **mobilization ratio of 0.82** — every federal dollar
pulled in $0.82 of additional non-federal capital. This site is the
companion to a working paper that asks one thing about that program:

> Does the credit mobilize private capital differently in rural versus
> urban markets — and if so, is the gap a *market-structure* phenomenon
> (rural markets are structurally less leverageable) or an
> *intermediary-selection* phenomenon (the CDEs allocated to rural deals
> are systematically less effective at mobilization)?

The headline finding, established by a layered fixed-effects regression
on every project the program has ever funded, is that **roughly 80% of
the aggregate rural mobilization gap is intermediary selection.** When
we hold the CDE constant — when the same intermediary deploys both
metro and non-metro deals in the same year and project type — the rural
penalty collapses to statistically zero. The rural gap is real; the
*market-structure* framing for it is mis-specified.

```{=html}
<div class="kpi-grid">
  <div class="kpi">
    <div class="lbl">Program years</div>
    <div class="val">2001–2022</div>
    <div class="sub">19,907 QLICIs · 8,024 projects</div>
  </div>
  <div class="kpi">
    <div class="lbl">Total QLICI deployed</div>
    <div class="val">$66.6 B</div>
    <div class="sub">nominal USD, CDE → QALICB</div>
  </div>
  <div class="kpi highlight">
    <div class="lbl">Mobilization ratio</div>
    <div class="val">0.82×</div>
    <div class="sub">private $ per federal $</div>
  </div>
  <div class="kpi">
    <div class="lbl">Non-metro $ share</div>
    <div class="val">19.6%</div>
    <div class="sub">vs. 20.0% statutory</div>
  </div>
  <div class="kpi">
    <div class="lbl">Median leverage, metro</div>
    <div class="val">1.19×</div>
  </div>
  <div class="kpi">
    <div class="lbl">Median leverage, non-metro</div>
    <div class="val">1.07×</div>
  </div>
  <div class="kpi highlight">
    <div class="lbl">Within-CDE rural penalty</div>
    <div class="val">≈ 0</div>
    <div class="sub">β = −0.05, p = 0.64</div>
  </div>
  <div class="kpi highlight">
    <div class="lbl">% of gap that is selection</div>
    <div class="val">~80%</div>
    <div class="sub">between-CDE component</div>
  </div>
</div>
```

## How to read this site

The chapters below are sequential but each stands alone. If you have
limited time:

- **For the punchline,** read [Results](prose/06-results.qmd) and
  [Interpretation](prose/07-interpretation.qmd) — about 12 minutes.
- **For the empirical strategy in detail,** add
  [Empirical strategy](prose/05-empirical-strategy.qmd) — another
  15 minutes.
- **For the explorer's view of the data,** open
  [The map](prose/04-the-map.qmd) — an interactive globe of every
  geocoded project, with year-range and metro filters.
- **For the full paper as a structured argument,** read
  [What is NMTC](prose/01-what-is-nmtc.qmd) through
  [Where this goes next](prose/10-next-steps.qmd) in order.

## Reproducibility

Every figure and every regression on this site is regenerated by a
single command from the SHA-256-anchored raw data:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/ihelfrich/us-nmtc-viewer
cd us-nmtc-viewer
pip install -r requirements.txt
python3 scripts/describe_nmtc.py        # raw .xlsx → cleaned CSVs
python3 scripts/make_figures.py         # → 6 PNG figures
python3 scripts/run_regressions.py      # → main_table.csv, bunching, etc.
```

Total runtime: about two minutes on a laptop. Source files, including
the original CDFI Fund release, live at
<https://github.com/ihelfrich/us-nmtc-viewer>.

## Citation

If you cite or reference this work, please use:

> Helfrich, I. T. (2026). *The Rural Mobilization Gap in U.S. Place-
> Based Tax Credit: Intermediary Selection vs. Market Structure in the
> New Markets Tax Credit.* Working paper.
> <https://nmtc.ianhelfrich.com>

The data is in the public domain (17 USC §105) per the CDFI Fund
disclosure. The analysis is © 2026 Dr. Ian Helfrich; the prose, figures,
and code are released under CC-BY-SA 4.0 with attribution.
